coredata.dc:title: A metal-free, dual catalyst for the removalof Rhodamine B using novel carbon quantum dots from muskmelon peel under sunlight and ultrasonication: A green way to clean the environment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Preethi, M.
Viswanathan, C.
Ponpandian, N.
coredata.dc:description: A green strategy for synthesizing fluorescent carbon quantum dots using muskmelon peel as a natural-based carbon precursor material was developed using a stirrer-assisted method for the application of catalytic degradation for Rhodamine B in the presence of sunlight and ultrasonication. For the first time, carbon source were made from the muskmelon peel, and it is used to produce ablue-fluorescent CQDs (carbon quantum dots), with a quantum yield (QY) of 39.51% and an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The CQDs have a sphericalmorphology and a uniform distribution, with an estimated diameter of about 3 nm. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission mainly due to a quantum confinement effect. Finally, we demonstrated that prepared CQDs could behave as an outstanding photocatalyst and a sonocatalyst to degrade the Rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The degradation efficiency of CQDs on RhB is99.11% under sunlight with a degradation rate constant of 0.06943 min−1 and 83.04% under ultrasonication. These findings suggest that the CQDs are an efficient catalyst for the breakdown of organic dyes in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis for CQDs
Muskmelon peel
Stirrer-assisted method
Degradation of Rhodamine B
Degradation efficiency

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: An instrument for the evaluation of characteristics of CQDs
Photocatalytic and sonocatalytic properties of prepared CQDs
Effect of catalyst concentration (CQDs) and dye concentration (Rhodamine B)
Effect of pH on degradation efficiency
Photocatalytic performance of CQDs catalyst on RhB
Effect of catalyst concentration (CQDs) and dye concentration (Rhodamine B)
Effect of pH on degradation efficiency
Effect of H2O2 concentration on degradation efficiency
Mechanism of photocatalytic and sonocatalytic activity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the process of preparing CQDs from muskmelon peel and the catalytic performance of CQDs.
(a) HRTEM image represents the uniformly distributed CQDs, (b) the particle size distribution curve reveals the average diameter of CQDs, (c) and (d) XPS survey and deconvoluted C1s spectrum of CQDs.

(a) The XRD pattern for CQDs shows a wide diffraction peak around 2θ = 18.61° (0.475 nm). (b) FTIR spectra for CQDs identify the existence of oxygen functional groups on the surface of CQDs. (c) The Raman spectra for CQDs around 1343 cm−1 (D band) and 1602 cm−1 (G band) and an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of CQD were 1.07, suggesting the structural defects induced by carbon disorder are related to the amorphous phase. (d) The absorption peak at 216 nm was determined to represent the transition for π-π* of a CC, while 280 nm was decided to constitute a transition for n-π* of the CO present in a muskmelon peel with occurring blue luminescence.
(a) The energy bandgap of CQDs is approximately 3.28 eV as determined by the Tauc plot. (b) A PL emission spectrum of CQDsat different wavelengths ranging from 330 nm to 380 nm.

(a) The block diagram was drawn between the degradation of RhB and the various concentration of CQDs catalyst (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, and 1.25 mg/mL) with different concentrations of RhB dye (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 µM/mL). The concentration of CQDs catalyst and RhB dye was 0.75 mg/mL, with 8 µM/mL has the maximum degradation rate of RhB. (b) The effect of pH was plotted between the degradation of RhB and different pH (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), and pH = 5 provides the maximumdegradation of RhB relative to other pH conditions. (c) The photocatalytic degradation for Rhodamine B dye was examined under the experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 0.75 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 8 µM/mL, pH – 5 and the dye intensity decreased with increasing irradiation time from 5 to 35 min with a degradation rate of 99.11%.

(a) A linear relationship between ln (C0/CT) and time (t) shows that photodegradation of RhB with a linear dependence (R2) was 0.994, and the rate constant (K) is 0.06943 min−1 under the experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 0.75 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 8 µM/mL, pH – 5 (b) TOC removal of complete dispersion during the degradation of RhB. (c) The block diagram reveals the reusability for CQDs in photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

(a) The block diagram was drawn between the degradation of RhB and the various concentration of CQDs catalyst (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, and 1.25 mg/mL) with different concentrations of RhB dye (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 µM/mL). The RhB dye and CQDs catalyst concentration was 10 µM/mL with 1 mg/mL with the maximum degradation rate of RhB. (b) The graph was plotted between the degradation of RhB and different pH (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), and pH = 3 provides the maximumdegradation of RhB relative to other pH conditions. (c)The sonocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B dye was examined under the experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 10 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 1 µM/mL, pH – 3, the dye intensity decreased with increasing irradiation time. The degradation process was complete in 100 min, with a degradation rate of 83.04%.

(a) The graph was plotted between the degradation of RhB and different H2O2 concentration (1 mM to 6 mM), and 3 mM has the maximumdegradation of RhB relative to H2O2 concentration under the experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 10 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 1 µM/mL, pH – 3, (b) TOC removal of complete dispersion during the degradation of RhB. (c) The block diagram reveals that the reusability for CQDs in sonocatalytic degradation of RhB.
Schematic representation of hypothesized pathway for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over CQDs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of TAOH caused by the interaction of TA with OH radicals generated by the CQDs catalyst. a) Under sunlight with the experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 0.75 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 8 µM/mL, pH – 5. b) Under ultra-sonication with experimental parameters of CQDs concentration – 10 mg/mL, RhB concentration – 1 µM/mL, pH – 3.

head.ce:title: A metal-free, dual catalyst for the removalof Rhodamine B using novel carbon quantum dots from muskmelon peel under sunlight and ultrasonication: A green way to clean the environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green strategy for synthesizing fluorescent carbon quantum dots using muskmelon peel as a natural-based carbon precursor material was developed using a stirrer-assisted method for the application of catalytic degradation for Rhodamine B in the presence of sunlight and ultrasonication. For the first time, carbon source were made from the muskmelon peel, and it is used to produce ablue-fluorescent CQDs (carbon quantum dots), with a quantum yield (QY) of 39.51% and an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The CQDs have a sphericalmorphology and a uniform distribution, with an estimated diameter of about 3 nm. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission mainly due to a quantum confinement effect. Finally, we demonstrated that prepared CQDs could behave as an outstanding photocatalyst and a sonocatalyst to degrade the Rhodamine B (RhB) dye. The degradation efficiency of CQDs on RhB is99.11% under sunlight with a degradation rate constant of 0.06943 min−1 and 83.04% under ultrasonication. These findings suggest that the CQDs are an efficient catalyst for the breakdown of organic dyes in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of the environment and society is still experiencing a lack of water problems, including contamination caused by organic and toxic chemicals [1,2]. The photocatalytic technology has recently become a sustainable green, one-step method for treating contamination efficiently and does not require additional materials [3]. The hazardous dyes derived from the textile industry generate large amounts of pollutants dumped every day into the ecosystem [4]. Also, Rhodamine B (RhB) is used in various industries, including textiles, leather, and the food processing industry. It is an extremely toxic dye that is carcinogenic and mutagenic to all living organisms [5]. Consequently, the researchers focus on finding alternative approaches for neutralizing these difficulties since these parameters have varying effects on the dye degradation rate: the pH, concentration, and light intensity. Specific traditional wastewater treatment methods for removing dye pollutants have many disadvantages, including low removal efficiencyagainst non-biodegradable or organic contaminants, a long period required to neutralize the dyes as well as post-treatment requirements [6].

The use of advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) to purify polluted wastewater has received considerable attention during the last decade. AOPs were described by a formation of ROS (reactive oxygen species), including OH (hydroxyl radical), H2O2 (hydrogen peroxide), and O2 (singlet oxygen), which are all capable of rapidly oxidizing dye molecules found within industrial pollutants. AOPs provide the Fenton reaction (which utilizes iron (Fe2+/ Fe3+) and H2O2) [7], sonocatalysis [8], photocatalysis [9], ozonation [10], sonolysis [11], and photolysis [12].

The sonolysis and photolysis have gained significant attention among advanced oxidation processes because it is environmentally friendly and became a successful technique for removing industrial dyes. In sonolysis, it requires an advantage for cavitation, a phenomenon caused by ultrasonic waves that generate growth and collapse of gas or liquid molecules. During Collapse, the free radicals (H· and OH·) are formed, rapidly oxidizing the organic dyes [13].

The redox potential is a measure of tendency of a redox couple to donate or accept electrons under standard conditions. When the bandgap of a semiconductor is close to the half redox potential of the H2O/OH, abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) will be generated which helps to breakdown the dye molecules.Despite their benefits and drawbacks, semiconductors are often used as photocatalysts for dye removal [14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained interest because they are inexpensive and environmentally friendly. They are non-toxic. They belong to the carbon nanomaterials group and represent a favorable alternative to certain toxic materials. In addition to these, CQDs have novel properties, including excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence, photostability, upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL), tunable fluorescence emission, excitation dependent photoluminescence, and low toxicity [15]. They mainly contain amorphous carbon regions and nanocrystalline regions of sp2/sp3-hybrid carbon, with various functional groups present on the surface of CQDs. Unlike other carbon nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes, the comparatively cheap and renewable resource of biomass sources, including watermelon rinds, cow manure, etc., can be used to produce CQDs.

The foremost reason behind emphasizing “Metal-free” is that no metal catalyst was used for the degradation process. To further confirm, we performed EDAX to obtain the elemental composition of muskmelon peel. The results revealed the presence of carbon (77.52%), oxygen (22.14%) and a trace amount of calcium (0.34%). The elemental mapping of those elements is presented in Fig. S1. The amount of calcium was negligible and during the processing of quantum dots like centrifuging, trace calcium will be removed, which is also confirmed in the XPS analysis of the prepared CQDs. As the dye degradation was mainly due to the catalytic effect of CQDs and no other metal helps in the degradation process, we claim that the catalyst used in this work is “Metal-free”.

Turning biomass waste into carbon nanostructures has gained a striking attention in the recent years in the global materials research community. Biomass waste derived carbon are used for various applications like sensors, super capacitors and pollutants absorbents. They are easy to prepare and moreover, adds value to a waste material. Among several possibilities, in this research we have explored the preparation and properties of CQDs prepared from muskmelon. Around 27.5 MTonnes of muskmelons are produced globally and its market is also growing worldwide. The peels of muskmelon are inconsumable and end up in food waste. Almost 1.3 billion tonnes of food waste is produced annually, most of which is not disposed properly. Using muskmelon peels as a source will help bring down the cost of preparation of biomass derived carbon nanomaterials.

Moreover, from EDAX, it can be seen that the muskmelon peels is mainly composed of Carbon and Oxygen alone, which makes it a reliable source for the production of CQDs. The goal of this study is to establish muskmelon peel as a useful bio-waste that can be converted into valuable carbon quantum dots, which can then be used in the degradation of Rhodamine B.

Many organic compounds now serve as suitable raw materials for developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and many methods exist. Still, the stirrer-assisted technique has the advantage of being inexpensive, easy, and can be carried out in an environmentally friendly manner. We used the stirrer-assisted technique to derive the CQDs from muskmelon peel for this work. To our knowledge, we are the first to have synthesized CQDs from muskmelon peel, and none of them has been previously reported. The as-prepared CQDs are used as the metal-free photocatalytic and sonocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B.

The musk melon was purchased from the supermarket (Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India). The chemicals and reagents utilized in this study were of analytical grade from Sigma-Aldrich and Himedia chemicals and were not purified prior to use. All aqueous solutions are processed using deionized water (DI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: An instrument for the evaluation of characteristics of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and particle size of the CQDs were determined through an X-ray diffraction pattern produced by a powder X-ray diffractometer system (Rigaku Smart Lab) completely loaded with CuKα radiation (1.5406 Å) at room temperature. The morphology of the CQDs was determined using an HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100). The elemental compositions of the CQDs were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (K-alpha surface analysis, Thermo Scientific). Utilizing Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy, the functional group of CQDs was determined (BRUKER, TENSOR 27). Raman spectroscopy was used to determine the chemical composition of the CQDs(Horiba Jobin Yvon Lab Ram-HR). Fluoromax-4, a spectrofluorometer, was used to measure the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum. For degradation, UV–visible absorption spectra were reported using a JASCO V-650 Spectrophotometer.

The CQDs were obtained from muskmelon peel using a stirrer-assisted method, as shown in Fig. 1 . To summarize, we picked fresh muskmelon from the supermarket and rinsed it with deionized water. These muskmelon peels were sliced into small pieces and mashed with a domestic mixer. The 30 mL of the muskmelon peel juice is combined with 25 mL of distilled water in a domestic mixer. The pH of this solution was 7.0, to accelerate the degradation rate, this was made acidic by adding HCl. Due to high oxidation and complex reactions, the solution changed from transparent to light brown after heating it at 70 °C for 5 h [16]. The aqueous solution was isolated from muskmelon peel using whatmann filter paper. The mixture was then sonicated and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the relatively large particles. This resulting mixture was filtered with Whatmann filter paper having a pore size of 0.2 μm and dried to produce CQDs.

The PLQY (photoluminescence quantum yield) was determined by comparing CQDs absorbance to the PL intensities employing quinine sulfate for the standard reference. An equation for PLQY is as follows:
(1)
Q
Y
s
= Q
Y
ST
I
S
I
S T
n
2
S
n
2
S T
A
S T
A
S

Here ‘S’ belongs to the CQDs and ‘ST’ belongs to the reference (quinine sulfate), ‘A’ represents the absorbance of areference andCQDs, ‘I’ indicate the correspondingPL intensity of the referenceand CQDs, and ‘η’ signifies the CQDs and reference's refractive index (both are 1.33). As a standard for CQDs, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a QYST of 54% was utilized.

The catalytic efficiency of CQDs was determined by measuring the rate at which Rhodamine B was degraded in the presence of sunlight and ultrasonication. The average sunlight and ultrasonic power utilized to degrade RhB are 25,000 LUX and 150 W. For the photocatalytic procedure, the catalyst CQDs with varied concentration (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, and 1.25 mg/mL) followed by the addition of a specific amount of Rhodamine B (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 µM/mL) were used for analysis.For the sonocatalytic process, the same amount of catalyst CQDs, the same concentration of RhB, followed the addition of H2O2 concentration (1 mM to 6 mM). After this, the solutionwas stirred for around 20 minto reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The experiment was conducted in the presence of sunlight and ultrasonication. Total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using an open reflux, titrimetric method outlined in standard methods. After the process, thesamples were taken, and it was measured byUV–vis spectrometer. The pH of corresponding CQDs solutions (0.02 mg/mL, 2 mL) were adjusted from acidic (3, 5) tobasic (9, 11) utilizing 1 M HCland 1 M NaOH, followed by 5 minof sonication, and the UV–vis spectrum was recorded.

The morphological characteristics of the CQDs extracted from the muskmelon peel gain the structural understanding illustrated in Fig. 2 (a). HRTEM image demonstrated the spherical shape and homogeneous dispersion without any indication of agglomeration for CQDs. The Image J software evaluates the particle size distribution of the CQDs, and it was found that the CQDs with an average diameter of 3 nm are inspected in Fig. 2(b).

The XPS survey spectrum results for the CQDs revealed two characteristic peaks at 283.2 and 531.3 eV, indicating that they were primarily made up of carbon and oxygen atoms, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The high-resolution deconvoluted C1S spectrum revealed three significant peaks at 284.75, 286.83, and 288.17 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, and CO, respectively Fig. 2 (d). It also provided strong evidence that CQDs have a graphite structure (sp2, CC) because the binding energy of graphite was 284.75 eV. The findings indicated that CQDs had an abundance of carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups in their molecular structure. Functional groups attached on the surface of CQDs exhibit high solubility in water.

Typically, the characteristic XRD pattern of CQD is seen in Fig. 3 (a). A wide peak was observed at 18.61° and 62.15°, respectively, and these peaks of diffraction correlate to a standard peak of graphite oxide [17]. The diffraction peak for CQDs had a maximum of 18.61° (d001 = 0.475 nm) due to the improved sp3 spacing between the carbon layers, which was similar to typical peak diffraction (2θ = 10.6°, (001) plane). Numerous functional groups have been identified and linked to the edges with base crystal planes, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and amino groups. A weak peak at 62.44° indicates amorphous carbon due to multiple functional groups and carbon graphitization.

The results also demonstrated that CQDs have a graphite composition, suggesting that carbon is arranged in a graphite framework. The decreasing intensity of the diffraction pattern peaks and increases with an FWHM (full-width half-maximum) owing to an amorphous carbon structure of preparation of CQDs. The weak crystalline characteristics were considered to be a distinguishing feature of these CQDs.

The FTIR spectrum was recorded in Fig. 3(b) to determine the functional groups present in the CQDs. In the spectral range of 3100–3600 cm−1, centered at 3376 cm−1, the FTIR spectrum revealed the band with other functional groups such as alcohols, phenols, and even water. The CH vibrational stretch was in the ranges of 2970 cm−1 and 2912 cm−1. Further, the absorption peaks at 1732 cm−1, 1602 cm−1, 1457 cm−1, and 1160 cm−1 exhibited the role of carboxyl and epoxy groups was revealed by CO, CC, COC, and CO vibration bands, respectively. Aromatic rings can be seen to exist with bands of 875–750 cm−1, centered at 864 cm−1 as assigned to out-of-plane CH bending vibrations. Because of their functional groups, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups have an excellent water solubility of synthesized carbon quantum dots from muskmelon peel.

The Raman spectrum of CQDs has two characteristic bands of 1343 and 1602 cm−1, corresponding to the D bandand G bandfor amorphous carbon, as indicated in Fig. 3(c). The G band can be attributed to sp2 carbon of an E2g vibration mode. In contrast, the D peak represents the graphite disorder, and it is related to the A1g vibration phonon, which is triggered primarily in the sp3 carbon defect. As a result, the ratios of intensity of a Defect band (ID) to the Graphite band (IG) were utilized to assess the qualitative nature of CQDs. CQDs have an intensity ratio of 1.07, indicating the structural defects caused by carbon disorder are correlated with the amorphous phase [18]. From analysis, the crystalline size for prepared CQDs from Raman analysis is ∼4.11 nm from the general formula for determining the crystallite size La using Tuinstra and Koenig’s law at an excitation laser of 514 nm [19]. The Raman analysis showed that the CQDs derived from muskmelon peel primarily comprise sp3 carbon defects with sp2 graphitic carbon and oxygen functional groups.

The optical characteristics of CQDs were examined using UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The CQDs have two distinct optical absorption peaks at 216 and 280 nm (Fig. 3 (d)), which correspond to the transition of π-π* of CC [20] and the transition of n-π* of CO [21] present in the muskmelon peel. The optical bandgap of semiconductors is determined using Tauc’s plot. The Tauc’s plot has the quantity hυ (photon energy) on the abscissa and (αhυ)r on the ordinate, where α is the material's absorption coefficient and r denotes the nature of the transition. The unit of αhυ is given as arbitraryunit (a.u.). The analysis shows that the energy bandgap of synthesized CQDs is around 3.28 eV using the Tauc plot, as seen in Fig. 4 (a). Additionally, quantum confinement theory postulates that holes in the valence band and electrons in the conduction band are bound or confined by the potential barriers on a quantum box's surface or potential well. As a result of the electrons-holes being confined, the energy bandgap between the valence and conduction bands increases as the particle size decreases [22].

A Prepared CQDs photoluminescence (PL) properties were investigated at various excitation wavelengths (λex) ranging from 330, 330, 340, 350, 360, and 380 nm. In Fig. 4 (b), the emission and excitation wavelengths for CQDs reach a maximum PL intensity at a wavelength of 440 nm, which corresponds to an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Additionally, the positions of emission peaks remained constant, suggesting the PL property of CQDs is excitation-independent.

A PLQY (photoluminescence quantum yield) of CQDs may be measured by calculating the absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) intensities for employing quinine sulfate as a standard reference. At a wavelength of 340 nm, a typical solution was optimized usingH2SO4 (0.1 M), resulting in a quantum yield for both standard and CQDs are 54% and 39.51%. A generic equation (PY = (m/M) × 100%) has been used to estimate a product yield (PY) forCQDs by multiplying the m (mass of the CQDs), M (mass of the fresh muskmelon peel), and a PY of prepared CQDs of 30 mL is ∼40.54%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and sonocatalytic properties of prepared CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of dye (Rhodamine B) using sunlight was used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of catalyst (CQDs). Whencatalyst (CQDs)are exposed to light with an energy greater than their band gap, an excited electron (e-) and a positive hole (h+) are generated, leading in the subsequent reduction and oxidation of these electrons by the e− and h+, respectively. In this way,the dye degradation process was initiated. It was found that the concentration of catalyst, dye, and pH all had an effect on the efficiency of dye degradation under sunlight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst concentration (CQDs) and dye concentration (Rhodamine B)
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the catalyst and dye has a significant effect on the overall rate of dye degradation. The degradation rate was investigated using a range of CQD catalyst concentrations (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, and 1.25 mg/mL) and RhB dye concentrations (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 µM/mL).The graph is shown in Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the relationship between various concentrations of CQDs catalyst and RhB dye in terms of degradation efficiency. The most efficient way to degrade RhB is using the CQD catalyst concentration (0.75 mg/mL) and RhB dye concentration (8 µM/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of a solution can play a massive impact in determining the rate of degradation of dye. The effect of photocatalytic degradation that changes the pH of CQD catalyst concentration (0.75 mg/mL) and RhB dye concentration (8 µM/mL) on RhB degradation has shown in Fig. 5(b). Five different pH values (initially 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) of CQDs catalyst were selected to examine the influence of pH. A solution was preparedby diluting the CQDs catalyst concentration of 0.75 mg/mL and the dye concentration of 8 µM/mL. Among these pH values, the pH = 5 has the removal efficiency for RhB was enhanced from 18.59% to 95.65%, while the irradiation time was increased from 5 to 35 min. As a result, we can observe that a minor change in the pH of the solution can affect the rate of RhB degradation. The observed and obtained findings for potential conjugation between CQDs and RhB in an acidic medium may be clarified because carboxylic acids do not undergo substitution reactions in a basic medium. Only deprotonation of the acid results in the formation of the carboxylate anion in this acidic medium. The pH of 5.0 has the maximum degradation of RhB compared to other pH conditions. Previously, the decrease in dye degradation efficiency above pH – 5 was caused mainly by repulsive forces between the CQDs catalyst and the RhB molecules [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of CQDs catalyst on RhB
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic degradation for CQDs catalyst on Rhodamine B dye was investigated in the existence of prepared CQDs as catalysts were evaluated under sunlight. The solution of 8 µM RhB dye is diluted in 100 mL of distilled water. Then we transferred the prepared RhB dye solution to the CQD solution, and the solution was constantly stirred over 20 min in dark conditions. The mixture was placed in direct sunlight throughout the afternoon (12–2 PM), and samples were collected in regular intervals of time (every 5 min). Using a UV–Vis spectrometer, the degradation efficiency of RhB dye can be determined from the collected samples. According to the Beer-Lambert law, the absorbance of RhB dye is proportional to the concentration for RhB dye; the degradation efficiency of RhB dye can be determined by
(2)
R =
(
C
0
- C )
/
C
0
×
100 %
where C0 and C be the CQDs catalyst concentration on RhB dye in time 0 and t. The degradation of RhB dye after exposure to prepared CQDs in sunlight at various time intervals is shown in Fig. 5(c). From the degradation process of RhB, the dye intensity decreased with increasing irradiation time. The degradation process was complete in 35 min, with a degradation rate of 99.11%, which improved the previous study's result [24]. This finding indicated that CQDs could be essential in photocatalytic applications.

A UV–Visible spectrum was used to investigate the photocatalytic effect of CQDs catalyst on RhB degradation after it was exposed to direct sunlight at various intervals to analyze it. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood dynamic model was used to study the reduction kinetics of RhB.
(3)
dc / d t = k K c /
1
+
K c
where c denotes a concentration at anytime, k and K denote the reaction's limiting rate constants at maximum coverage under specified experimental conditions, and signifies the equilibrium constant. When the substrate concentration is less than one, Eq. (3) can be transformed into a pseudo-first-order kinetic equation (as seen in Eq. (4)).
ln (
C 0
/
C T
) = k t

C0 and CT denote the equilibrium adsorption and RhB concentrations at time t, respectively, and k denotes the apparent rate constant. Thus, the rate of reaction is independentof the concentration of C0. Linear fitting of an ln(C0/CT) – time plot provides a slopeequal to the photocatalytic degradation reaction rate. The plot of ln(C0/CT) versus time for prepared photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 6 (a). The linear relationship between ln(C0/CT) and t implies that photodegradation of RhB on these photocatalysts follows the LH dynamic model and first-order reaction kinetics. Additionally, the finding shows that the photocatalytic degradation of RhB follows pseudo-first-order kinetics, with a linear dependence (R2) is 0.994 and the rate constant (K) of 0.06943 min−1.

The total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was utilized to determine the mineralization of the dye, as shown in Fig. 6(b). The CQDs catalyst and RhB dye solution originated with a TOC value of 0.113%, but after 35 min, it had dropped to 0.011%. It was observed that the TOC reduction of RhB dye solution occurred within 35 min of visible light in the presence of CQDs catalyst, confirming that RhB dye solution had been photodegraded [25]. The reusability of CQDs catalyst in photocatalytic degradationof RhB was illustrated in Fig. 6(c). The photocatalytic degradation was measured at 99.11%, but it was 99.01% after five cycles. As a result, it was established that CQDs exhibit superior stability and reusability in the presence of sunlight.

The sonocatalytic activity of catalyst (CQDs) was evaluated by degrading dye (Rhodamine B) using the ultrasonication. By applying the ultrasound to a chemical reaction (CQDs + dye) induces specific activation based on a physical phenomenon, which is acoustic cavitation. Cavitation is a process in which mechanical activation destroys the attractive forces of molecules in the liquid phase. As a result, the dye degradation process was initiated. The effects of various experimental parameters including the concentration of catalyst, dye, H2O2 and pH on the efficiency of dye degradation through sonication were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of catalyst concentration (CQDs) and dye concentration (Rhodamine B)
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of the catalyst and dye has a considerable impact on the degradation rate of the dye. The different CQDs catalyst concentration (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1.25 mg/mL) and dye concentration (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 mg/mL) were used to examine the concentrations on both CQDs catalyst and RhB on the degradation rate of RhB dye. From Fig. 7 (a), it can be concluded that concentrations of CQDs are 1 mg/mL and RhB are 10 µM/mL have the highest degradation efficiency than other different concentrations of CQDs on RhB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The pH plays a significant role in the rate of degradation of dyes. Five different pH (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) of CQDs catalyst were selected to explore the impact of pH. It is added to aqueous solutions containing the respective concentrations of CQDs catalyst concentration (1 mg/mL) and RhB dye concentration (10 mg/mL) and in Fig. 7(b). The degradation efficiency was found to have no noticeable impact on the degradation rate at various pH values. However, thesonocatalytic degradation is faster in an acidic environment than in a neutral or basic environment. Among these pH values, pH = 3.0 improved the removal efficiency of RhB from 20.37 to 79.04% while increasing the irradiation time from 5 to 100 min. The pH of 3.0 is optimal for providing the most effective degradation efficiency from other pH conditions.

The sonocatalytic degradation of CQDs as a catalyst was studied under Rhodamine B dye. The addition of RhB dye to DI water, diluting it by 8 µM and transferring it to the CQDs solution, which was then stirred for 20 min in the dark condition. Throughout the 100 min, the mixture was sonicated, and samples were collected at 5-minute intervals. The degradation efficiency of RhB dye was measured, and the dye intensity decreased with increasing irradiation time. The degradation process was completed in 100 min, with a degradation rate of 83.04%. The degradation of RhB dye was analyzed at various time intervals in Fig. 7(C). This observation indicates that CQDs are critical in sonocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of H2O2 concentration on degradation efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of H2O2 concentration is crucial because it influences the production of OH radicals. To investigate the degradation of RhB dye, the H2O2 concentration was varied from 1 mM to 6 mM under the concentration of CQDs (1 mg/mL) and dye concentration (10 mg/mL). Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the effect of H2O2 on RhB dye degradation. The degradation rate of RhB dye increased when the H2O2 concentration was raised from 1 mM to 3 mM and subsequently dropped. The maximum RhB dye degradation rate was determined to be 83.04% at 3 mM, respectively. Additionally, at higher H2O2 concentrations (4 mM), the decrease in RhB dye degradation may be due to scavenging of produced OH radicals, i.e., the OH radicals may react with H2O2 to form HO2 radicals, which are less reactive than OH radicals.

The total organic carbon (TOC) analysis was utilized to determine the mineralization of the dye, as shown in Fig. 8(b). The CQDs and RhB dye solution originated with a TOC value of 0.113%, but after 100 min, it had dropped to 0.027%. It was observed that the TOC reduction of RhB dye solution occurred within 100 min in the presence of CQDs, confirming that RhB dye solution had been photodegraded [25]. Theexperiments were performed on the reusability of CQDs in sonocatalytic degradationof RhB to obtain theresults in Fig. 8(c). The sonocatalytic degradation was measured at 83.04%, but it was 83.00% after five cycles. As a result, it was established that CQDs exhibit superior stability and reusability in the presence of ultrasonication. We performed the sonocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B without CQDs. Fig. S2 compares the degradation of RhB with and without CQDs under sonication. It can be seen that there is no obvious change in the RhB solution without CQDs but in the presence of CQDs, the degradation of RhB takes place. This result confirms the sonocatalytic degradation of RhB by employing CQDs as a catalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic and sonocatalytic activity

ce:section.ce:para: The Fig. 9 illustrates a hypothesized pathway for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over CQDs. WhenCQDs photocatalyst are exposed to visible light with photon energies larger than or equal to their bandgaps, the electrons in the valence band of CQDs are stimulated to the conduction band, forming free electron-hole pairs. The charge carriers created by photons can move to the surface of CQDs. The photoexcited electrons were transported to the adsorbed O2, which was then reduced to O2 –, generating highly oxidizing peroxy species. On the other hand, photogenerated holes in the valence band can be readily trapped by surface-adsorbed hydroxyl groups and water molecules, resulting in the generation of OH radicals on the surface of CQDs.

Following that, these OH radicals can diffuse into solution phase and damage the chromophoric structure of theRhB, culminating theirdegradation of RhB. To explore this challenge, we used a terephthalic acid (TA) technique to detect the produced OH radicals. The study demonstrated that when the OH reacts with TA, a fluorescent molecule of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with an emission peak centered at about 425 nm is formed [26]. As illustrated in Fig. 10 a, when the CQDs photocatalyst placed in TA solution are exposed to visible light, the fluorescence attributed with TAOH increases with increasing irradiation time, implying that the photogenerated holes in the CQDs' valence band have sufficient driving force to oxidize surface-adsorbed hydroxyl groups and water molecules to generate OH radicals. Finally, these active species may oxidize dye molecules adsorbed on the active sites of CQDs, resulting in dye degradation and the generation of CO2, H2O, and other byproducts.

Organic contaminants may be sonodegraded in aqueous solution through two mechanisms: (i) direct pyrolysis in the gas phase of cavitation bubbles caused by high-localized temperature and pressure, or (ii) interactions with free radicals in a bubble interfacial region. During the collapse of the cavitation bubble, free OH, H, and HO2 radicals are generated due to the thermolysis of water molecules. The sono-excited electrons on the surface of CQDs may be grabbed by H2O2 and O2 in the solution, forming OH and O2 radicals, respectively.

Following that, these OH radicals can diffuse into solution phase and damage the chromophoric structure of theRhB, culminating theirdegradation of RhB. To explore this challenge, we used a terephthalic acid (TA) technique to detect the produced OH radicals. The study demonstrated that when the OH reacts with TA, a fluorescent molecule of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with an emission peak centered at about 426 nm is formed [26]. As illustrated in Fig. 10b, when the CQDs photocatalyst placed in TA solution are exposed to visible light, the fluorescence attributed with TAOH increases with increasing irradiation time, implying that the photogenerated holes in the CQDs' valence band have sufficient driving force to oxidize surface-adsorbed hydroxyl groups and water molecules to generate OH radicals. Finally, these active species may oxidize dye molecules adsorbed on the active sites of CQDs through electrostatic attraction, resulting in dye degradation and the generation of CO2, H2O2, and other byproducts.

In brief, a feasible and inexpensive technique has been developed to synthesis the fluorescent CQDs using muskmelon peel as a carbon source through a stirrer-assisted technique. The precursor becomes cost-efficient while overcoming toxic effects due to widely accessible fruit waste as a carbon precursor. The prepared CQDs have a spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution of approximately 3 nm. The CQDs have an amorphous structure and contain carboxyl and other oxygen-containing groups. They show strong blue fluorescent CQDs with a quantum yield (QY) of 39.51% at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Such interesting properties render the CQDs havean excellent catalyst for RhB degradation in sunlight and ultrasonication. The optimized concentration ofmain parameters for CQDs catalyst and RhB dye are 0.75 mg/mL, 8 µM/mL and pH = 5for photocatalytic degradation, and 1 mg/mL, 10 µM/mL, pH = 3, and H2O2 = 3 mM for sonocatalytic degradation. The degradation efficiency of CQDs catalyst is99.11% on RhB after 35 min in sunlight with a degradation rate constant of 0.06943 min−1 and 83.04% after 100 min in ultrasonication. To explore this challenge, we used a terephthalic acid (TA) technique to detect the produced OH radicals. The study demonstrated that when the OH reacts with TA, a fluorescent molecule of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) with an emission peak centered at about 425 nm is formed. The prepared CQDs are an excellent alternative for a green catalyst due to their versatility in photocatalytic and sonocatalytic activity.

M. Preethi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing-original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. C. Viswanathan: Validation, Formal analysis. N. Ponpandian: Conceptualization, Validation, Review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the DST-FIST; DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP, Government of India for the instrumental facilities in the department.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113765.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
